title: Information releases, embargoes, and sanctions | Stats NZ
content:
## Information releases, embargoes, and sanctions
Most of Stats NZ’s information releases are made public to all New Zealanders at the same time, generally 10:45am. The release calendar shows when releases will be available.
- Sanctions if embargoes are broken
- Secure briefings (secure media conferences) for market-sensitive releases
- Secure media conferences
- Registers of those who attend secure briefings for market-sensitive releases
- Briefings for ministers during COVID-19 lockdowns
- Releases provided under embargo before publication
Generally, there is no prior access for any stakeholders, including government ministers. However, some ‘market-sensitive’ or ‘topical releases’ may be available for advance briefing under embargoed conditions set under the Data and Statistics Act 2022 .
Embargoed conditions may include that the information is not broadcast, published, or disclosed to another person for a period of time specified by the Government Statistician.
Such access is at the discretion of the Government Statistician. It is provided to ensure stakeholders and commentators are well informed and to promote the value of the data and its story.
Stats NZ is required by the Data and Statistics Act 2022 to publish information about who has access to embargoed information.

## Sanctions if embargoes are broken
Stats NZ takes security at lock-up briefings extremely seriously. The information provided is potentially market-sensitive and could move currency or other financial markets if the embargo is broken.
This sensitivity means that anyone who breaks the embargo faces serious sanctions.
Failure to comply with embargo conditions is a criminal offence and may result in a fine of up to $5,000 for an individual or $15,000 for an organisation.
Other sanctions for breaking the embargo include:
- the individual and organisation may be named publicly
- the individual and organisation may be barred from future access to embargoed information
- concerns may be raised with relevant law enforcement, regulatory and/or professional organisations.
Sanctions are at the discretion of the Government Statistician (or delegate).

## Secure briefings (secure media conferences) for market-sensitive releases
The key market-sensitive releases are:
- gross domestic product
- balance of payments and international investment position
- consumers price index
- labour market statistics (unemployment).
Only known and trusted media, bank economists, opposition party advisers, and government agency representatives are given embargoed access to market-sensitive releases.
Secure briefings for embargoed releases are held at Stats NZ offices in Wellington and Auckland in the hour before public release. They are run under strict physical ‘lock-up’ conditions.
Responsible ministers and their key advisers are also briefed on these releases in a secure room at Parliament in the hour before release.

## Secure media conferences
Those attending ‘lock-up’ media conferences must surrender their mobile phones at the door and are forbidden to contact anyone outside.
The following guidelines are supplied when people attend a media conference.
- Once you have entered the room you must not leave until 10.45am when the embargo is lifted.
- Please sign in, turn off your mobile phones, including smartphones, and put them in the compartment at the conference room entrance. Having your phone in flight mode is a violation of our required protocols. It must be powered off.
- Having your phone in flight mode is a violation of our required protocols. It must be powered off.
- Laptop external wireless communication devices must be placed beside the laptop on the media room table for the duration of the media conference, and cannot be connected to the computer until the embargo is lifted.
- Laptops or other devices that have internal wireless network capability must not connect or transmit until the embargo has been lifted.
- We reserve the right to inspect devices to ensure that internal wireless capability is turned off.

## Briefings for ministers during COVID-19 lockdowns
During national and regional lockdowns for the COVID-19 pandemic, we were not able to provide secure briefings in person for embargoed releases at our offices in Wellington and Auckland or at Parliament. Instead, we sent embargoed documents by secure email to ministers responsible for economic issues in their portfolio and their key advisers in the hour before the release of key economic data.

### Registers of parliamentary staff who received the briefings
- Register of parliamentary staff who received Labour market statistics: March 2020 quarter under embargo Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 151 KB

### Register of parliamentary staff who received Labour market statistics: March 2020 quarter under embargo
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 151 KB

## Releases provided under embargo before publication
Some topical releases, including joint releases with other agencies, may be the subject of intense public debate and require an immediate response from ministers and/or key stakeholders. These are typically complex, longer-form reports, or detailed releases covering new information.
Such reports are not market-sensitive but may include topics such as environmental reports, child poverty, injury, or property transfers.
At the discretion of the Government Statistician, topical releases may be made available to relevant and responsible ministers, key stakeholders and the media up to 24 hours before the release is made public. The Government Statistician also has discretion to allow restricted access to releases earlier than 24 hours before release.
A strict embargo applies to these releases. Stakeholders who receive the releases ahead of time, including government agencies, national sector interest groups, and media, may not communicate the contents to others. Sanctions apply to anyone who breaks the embargo.
